title: Communication Signals
summary: Communication signals track how language, media, platforms, and trust are changing in public and professional life.

items.name: AI drafting tools are normalising
items.summary: People are using models to write and summarise more often.
items.whyItMatters: Communication now includes human editing of machine output.

items.name: Attention is more fragmented
items.summary: Short-form and platform algorithms are changing how messages are received.
items.whyItMatters: The channel now shapes the message more than ever.

items.name: Media platforms
items.whyItMatters: Platform incentives influence what communication wins.

items.name: PR and comms firms
items.summary: Professional communication is now strategic infrastructure.
items.whyItMatters: Message design has become a business function.

items.name: Newsrooms
items.whyItMatters: The institution of journalism remains central to public trust.

items.name: Universities
items.summary: Communication research continues to shape the field.
items.whyItMatters: Academic theory informs both media and work communication.

items.name: Chip Heath
items.summary: A clear writer on sticky ideas.
items.whyItMatters: His work is useful wherever clarity matters.

items.name: Barbara Minto
items.whyItMatters: Her framework is still widely used in business writing.

items.name: Can AI improve writing without flattening voice?
items.summary: Tools can accelerate drafting but also homogenise style.

items.name: How do we restore trust online?
items.summary: Verification, provenance, and platform design all matter.
items.whyItMatters: Communication is now partly an infrastructure problem.
